title: Detrended fluctuation analysis and related methods
text: Detrended Fluctuation Analysis (DFA) relatives family methods measuring long-range correlations cross-correlations nonstationary time series, classical autocorrelation ordinary regression biased trends. DFAmethods2 collects useful members family common, consistent interface: Several regression-oriented tools — scale-dependent standardized coefficients, scale-wise f2f^2effect size intersection-union test — introduced Barreto et al. (2021), whose framework package implements.
code: library(DFAmethods2)

title: Detrended fluctuation analysis and related methods
text: Except dfa() (takes single numeric vector), functions expect matrix data frame whose first column response YY whose remaining columns covariates X2,…,XpX_2, \dots, X_p. share four arguments: dpo — order polynomial used detrending (1 = linear); int — TRUE, series integrated (cumulatively summed) first; np — number scales (box sizes) statistics evaluated; overlap — TRUE, sliding (overlapping) boxes used. Throughout vignette use simulated system response y depends two random-walk drivers, x2 (positive) x3 (negative):
code: n  <- 1200 x2 <- cumsum(rnorm(n)) x3 <- cumsum(rnorm(n)) y  <- 0.7 * x2 - 0.5 * x3 + cumsum(rnorm(n)) dat <- data.frame(y = y, x2 = x2, x3 = x3)

title: Detrended fluctuation analysis and related methods
text: DFA (Peng et al. 1994) quantifies scaling fluctuations series. series first integrated profile X(t)=∑=1t(xi−x‾)X(t) = \sum_{=1}^{t} (x_i - \bar{x}), split boxes size ss; within box polynomial trend removed variance residuals taken. Averaging boxes gives fluctuation function F(s)F(s), self-similar series follows power law F(s)∼sα, F(s) \sim s^{\alpha},  exponent α\alpha generalises Hurst exponent: α=0.5\alpha = 0.5 marks uncorrelated series, α>0.5\alpha > 0.5 persistence (long-range positive correlations) α<0.5\alpha < 0.5 anti-persistence. plotdfa() draws log–log fluctuation plot fitted exponent:
code: fy <- dfa(dat$y, np = 40) head(fy) #> # A tibble: 6 × 2 #>       s     F #>   <int> <dbl> #> 1    10  3.81 #> 2    11  5.01 #> 3    12  6.43 #> 4    13  8.09 #> 5    14 10.0  #> 6    15 12.2  # the DFA exponent is the slope of log F(s) against log s alpha <- coef(lm(log10(fy$F) ~ log10(fy$s)))[[2]] round(alpha, 3) #> [1] 3.014 plotdfa(fy, main = "DFA of y")

title: Detrended fluctuation analysis and related methods
text: Detrended Cross-Correlation Analysis (DCCA) (Podobnik Stanley 2008) replaces box variance detrended covariance FXY2(s)F_{XY}^2(s) two integrated profiles. FXY2(s)F_{XY}^2(s) unbounded, Zebende (2011) normalised DCCA cross-correlation coefficient ρDCCA(s)=FXY2(s)FX(s)FY(s)∈[−1,1], \rho_{\mathrm{DCCA}}(s) = \frac{F_{XY}^2(s)}{F_X(s)\,F_Y(s)} \[-1, 1],  scale--scale analogue Pearson’s correlation robust trends. rhodcca() returns one column per pair series:  coefficient strongly positive y–x2 pair, built simulation.

title: Detrended fluctuation analysis and related methods
text: several series interact, direct ρDCCA\rho_{\mathrm{DCCA}} may inflated shared drivers. Detrended Partial-Cross-Correlation Analysis (Yuan et al. 2015) removes linear influence remaining series inverting ρDCCA\rho_{\mathrm{DCCA}} matrix scale, giving partial coefficient ρDPCCA(s)\rho_{\mathrm{DPCCA}}(s):

title: Detrended fluctuation analysis and related methods
text: Detrended Multiple Cross-Correlation Coefficient (Zebende Silva Filho 2018) generalises multiple-correlation coefficient (RR) scales. Writing 𝛒(s)\boldsymbol{\rho}(s) matrix ρDCCA(s)\rho_{\mathrm{DCCA}}(s) among covariates 𝛒Y⋅(s)\boldsymbol{\rho}_{Y\cdot}(s) coefficients response, DMC(s)=𝛒Y⋅(s)⊤𝛒(s)−1𝛒Y⋅(s)∈[0,1], \mathrm{DMC}(s) = \boldsymbol{\rho}_{Y\cdot}(s)^{\top}\,                   \boldsymbol{\rho}(s)^{-1}\,                   \boldsymbol{\rho}_{Y\cdot}(s) \[0, 1],  measuring much response jointly explained covariates scale (recent statistical test given Wang, Xu, Fan (2021)):
code: dm <- dmc2(dat, np = 40) plot(dm[[1]], dm[[2]], type = "b", pch = 19, ylim = c(0, 1),      xlab = "scale s", ylab = "DMC(s)", main = "Detrended multiple correlation")

title: Detrended fluctuation analysis and related methods
text: Kristoufek (2015) turned detrended (co)variances full regression framework. Collecting detrended covariance matrix 𝐅(s)\mathbf{F}(s), scale-wise regression coefficients 𝛃(s)=𝐅XX(s)−1𝐅XY(s), \boldsymbol{\beta}(s) = \mathbf{F}_{XX}(s)^{-1}\,\mathbf{F}_{XY}(s),  unstandardised effect covariate response scale ss , unlike OLS, can vary across scales. multivariate case (p≥2p \ge 2 covariates, detrended multiple linear regression) due Shen (2015). betadfa() returns coefficients sbdfa() standardised counterparts (computed ρDCCA\rho_{\mathrm{DCCA}} matrices), Barreto et al. (2021) proposed scale-wise measure relative importance:  estimates hover around true coefficients 0.70.7 −0.5-0.5 every scale. fracreg() --one workhorse: single call returns scale vector, detrended (partial) correlations, raw standardised β(s)\beta(s), coefficient determination, DMC, confidence limits pp-values:
code: b  <- betadfa(dat, np = 40) head(b) #> # A tibble: 6 × 3 #>       s    x2     x3 #>   <int> <dbl>  <dbl> #> 1    10 0.577 -0.441 #> 2    11 0.568 -0.436 #> 3    12 0.561 -0.432 #> 4    13 0.555 -0.428 #> 5    14 0.550 -0.424 #> 6    15 0.545 -0.420  matplot(b$s, b[, c("x2", "x3")], type = "l", lty = 1, lwd = 2,         xlab = "scale s", ylab = expression(beta(s)),         main = "Scale-wise regression coefficients") abline(h = c(0.7, -0.5), lty = 3, col = "grey50") legend("right", c("x2", "x3"), col = 1:2, lty = 1, lwd = 2, bty = "n") fr <- fracreg(dat, dpo = 1, int = TRUE, np = 40) names(fr) #>  [1] "s"       "F"       "DCCA"    "DPCCA"   "BDFA"    "BSDFA"   "UDFA"    #>  [8] "VDFA"    "VDFA2"   "DMC2"    "R2DFA"   "UCIB"    "LCIB"    "p.value" #> [15] "TC"

title: Detrended fluctuation analysis and related methods
text: effsizeDFA() reports scale-wise effect size introduced Barreto et al. (2021) — Cohen’s f2f^2(Cohen 1988) evaluated scale, f2=(Rfull2−Rreduced2)/(1−Rfull2)f^2 = (R^2_{\text{full}} - R^2_{\text{reduced}}) / (1 - R^2_{\text{full}}) — turning regression scale-resolved importance measure:

title: Detrended fluctuation analysis and related methods
text: DFAmethods2 ships surrogate-based tests scale-wise coefficients, built phase-randomised surrogates series: fracreg.PStest() — Podobnik–Shen test H0:β(s)=0H_0\!: \beta(s) = 0 (detrended effect) (Podobnik et al. 2011; Shen 2015); fracreg.Ktest() — Kristoufek test H0:βDFA(s)=βOLSH_0\!: \beta_{\mathrm{DFA}}(s) = \beta_{\mathrm{OLS}} (scale effect equals global OLS one) (Kristoufek 2015); fracreg.IUTest() — intersection–union test Barreto et al. (2021) (following intersection–union principle, e.g. Van Deun et al. (2009)), combines two reject covariate direct, scale-dependent effect OLS miss. returns estimated β(s)\beta(s) together lower/upper critical bounds test, coefficient significant scale falls outside band. use small number surrogates speed; raise B practice.  x2 coefficient lies well null band scales — clear, scale-consistent effect.
code: ps <- fracreg.PStest(dat, B = 30, np = 30) head(ps) #> # A tibble: 6 × 7 #>    bet1   bet2  slci1  slci2 suci1 suci2     s #>   <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <int> #> 1 0.577 -0.441 -0.269 -0.419 0.297 0.217    10 #> 2 0.568 -0.436 -0.282 -0.436 0.319 0.223    11 #> 3 0.561 -0.432 -0.292 -0.448 0.335 0.226    12 #> 4 0.555 -0.428 -0.298 -0.456 0.343 0.227    13 #> 5 0.550 -0.424 -0.311 -0.459 0.344 0.226    14 #> 6 0.545 -0.420 -0.328 -0.458 0.339 0.223    15 plot(ps$s, ps$bet1, type = "l", lwd = 2,      ylim = range(ps[, c("bet1", "slci1", "suci1")]),      xlab = "scale s", ylab = expression(beta[x2](s)),      main = "x2 coefficient vs Podobnik-Shen null band") lines(ps$s, ps$slci1, lty = 2, col = "red") lines(ps$s, ps$suci1, lty = 2, col = "red")

title: Detrended fluctuation analysis and related methods
text: Choose np implied range box sizes care: small boxes noisy large boxes leave windows. defaults span box sizes 10 one fifth series length. Use int = TRUE noise-like (stationary) inputs int = FALSE series already integrated (random-walk-like). surrogate tests computationally heavy (refit model B times); start small increase B workflow settled.

what: Detrended Fluctuation Analysis and Related Methods
title: Detrended Fluctuation Analysis and Related Methods
text: DFAmethods2 toolbox Detrended Fluctuation Analysis (DFA) methods measuring long-range correlations, cross-correlations regression nonstationary time series, classical autocorrelation ordinary regression biased trends. provides: Detrended Fluctuation Analysis — dfa() Detrended Cross-Correlation Analysis rho-DCCA coefficient — rhodcca() Detrended Partial Cross-Correlation coefficient — rhodpcca() Detrended Multiple Cross-Correlation coefficient (DMC) — dmc2() Detrended fractal regression: scale-wise standardized coefficients — betadfa(), sbdfa(), fracreg() Scale-wise f2f^2effect sizes — effsizeDFA() Significance tests: Podobnik-Shen, Kristoufek intersection-union — fracreg.PStest(), fracreg.Ktest(), fracreg.IUTest() scale-dependent standardized coefficients, scale-wise effect size intersection-union test follow Barreto et al. (2021) doi:10.1016/j.physa.2021.126259.

title: Detrended Fluctuation Analysis and Related Methods
code: # install.packages("devtools") devtools::install_github("Ikarobarreto/DFAmethods2")

title: Detrended Fluctuation Analysis and Related Methods
text: response y driven covariate x2, random-walk-like: See vignette("DFAmethods2") full theory worked examples.
code: library(DFAmethods2) #> Registered S3 method overwritten by 'quantmod': #>   method            from #>   as.zoo.data.frame zoo  set.seed(1) x2  <- cumsum(rnorm(800)) y   <- 0.7 * x2 + cumsum(rnorm(800)) dat <- data.frame(y = y, x2 = x2)  # DFA fluctuation function of y head(dfa(y, np = 30)) #> # A tibble: 6 × 2 #>       s     F #>   <int> <dbl> #> 1    10  3.50 #> 2    11  4.65 #> 3    12  6.03 #> 4    13  7.68 #> 5    14  9.62 #> 6    15 11.9  # scale-wise rho-DCCA cross-correlation coefficient head(rhodcca(dat, np = 30)) #> # A tibble: 6 × 2 #>       s DCCA12 #>   <int>  <dbl> #> 1    10  0.487 #> 2    11  0.471 #> 3    12  0.457 #> 4    13  0.445 #> 5    14  0.434 #> 6    15  0.426
